data.content:
Now I have the full picture. Let me create the `Directory.Packages.props` and update all project files.

**Version conflicts resolved (highest wins):**
- `System.Text.Json`: 8.0.4 vs 10.0.1 → **10.0.1**
- `Azure.Identity`: 1.11.4 vs 1.13.2 → **1.13.2**

arguments.file_text:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersions>true</ManagePackageVersions>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageVersion Include="Azure.Identity" Version="1.13.2" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting" Version="1.15.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="10.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.9.3" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I understand the full picture. Let me collect all packages and their versions:

- System.Text.Json: 10.0.1 (Api), 8.0.4 (Worker), 10.0.1 (Shared), 10.0.1 (Tests) → highest: 10.0.1
- Azure.Identity: 1.13.2 (Api), 1.11.4 (Shared) → highest: 1.13.2
- Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting: 8.0.1 (Worker) → 8.0.1
- OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting: 1.15.0 (Worker) → 1.15.0


- xunit: 2.9.3 (Tests) → 2.9.3

Now I need to set up centralized package management by creating a Directory.Packages.props file at the solution root that enables version management and declares all these packages at their highest versions, then strip out the version attributes from each project's package references.

  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
